messages.content:
### Summary: Algebraic, Analytic, and Computational Number Theory

This special issue, published in the journal *Mathematics*, presents a collection of research articles bridging theoretical foundations and practical applications in number theory. 

#### **Core Disciplines**
*   **Analytic Number Theory:** Focuses on the distribution of primes, the Riemann zeta function, and the use of Dirichlet series and L-functions to solve problems concerning integers [S.10, S.12].
*   **Algebraic Number Theory:** Investigates the arithmetic of algebraic number fields, including the study of unique factorization, ideal class groups, and the group of units in rings of integers [S.10, S.12].
*   **Computational & Applied Number Theory:** Explores practical implementations, including authentication protocols based on Diophantine equations [S.14], cryptographic applications [S.14], and the study of specialized mathematical sequences and structures [S.13, S.14].

#### **Research Highlights**
The collection features interdisciplinary contributions from global research centers, categorized by these primary themes:
*   **Sequences and Quaternions:** Several papers analyze generalized Fibonacci and Lucas sequences, as well as new families of quaternions (e.g., Pauli-Gaussian Fibonacci quaternions) and their associated identities [S.13, S.14].
*   **Algebraic Structure & L-Functions:** Studies include new zero-density results for L-functions of $GL(n)$ [S.13], properties of residuated lattices [S.14], and regularity in ordered n-ary semihypergroups [S.13].
*   **Computational Applications:** Contributors explored the density of sequences modulo 1, the classification of telescopic numerical semigroups, and the development of new authentication protocols utilizing quadratic Diophantine equations, highlighting the intersection of abstract theory and modern cryptography [S.14, S.15].

#### **Editorial Goal**
The editors—Diana Savin, Nicusor Minculete, and Vincenzo Acciaro—curated this volume to foster connectivity between pure research and diverse fields such as associative algebra, logical algebras, combinatorics, and difference equations, reflecting the broad utility of modern number theory [S.10, S.12].

Lesson 4 — Number Theory and Applications - Presentation3 slides
Number Theory and Applications
  • Explores algebraic, analytic, and computational methods.
  • Covers research on arithmetic structures and number sequences.

This document is a reprint of a Special Issue published in the journal *Mathematics*, focusing on the fields of algebraic, analytic, and computational number theory [S.1, S.5].

**Key Points:**

*   **Editorial Team:** The volume is edited by Diana Savin and Nicusor Minculete from the Transilvania University of Brasov, Romania, and Vincenzo Acciaro from the Chieto-Pescara University, Italy [S.5].
*   **Publication Context:** It compiles various research articles originally published in the open-access journal *Mathematics* (ISSN 2227-7390) [S.5].
*   **Access and Licensing:** The book is available in both hardback and PDF formats. While individual articles are published under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license, the compilation as a whole is distributed by MDPI under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs (CC BY-NC-ND) license [S.5].
*   **Identification:** The work is identified by ISBN 978-3-0365-9859-8 (Hbk) and ISBN 978-3-0365-9860-4 (PDF), with a digital object identifier available at doi.org/10.3390/books978-3-0365-9860-4 [S.5].

This document contains the table of contents, preface, and biographies for a collection of research articles titled "Algebraic, Analytic, and Computational Number Theory and Its Applications," published in *Mathematics* (2024) [S.6].

### Key Content Overview
The compilation includes nineteen research papers published between 2021 and 2023 covering a wide range of topics in number theory and mathematics:

Significant theoretical studies focus on number sequences, such as generalized Lucas pseudoprimality [S.6], Fibonacci numbers of order $r$ [S.6], Pauli Gaussian quaternions [S.7], and $k$-Fibonacci numbers [S.7].

Algebraic and computational contributions include investigations into class number divisibility in imaginary quadratic fields [S.6], indices of septic number fields [S.7], and the classification of telescopic numerical semigroups [S.7].

Applied mathematics research is represented by new authentication protocols based on quadratic Diophantine equations [S.6] and the use of generalized Fibonacci sequences in elliptic curve cryptography [S.7].

Analytic and auxiliary studies cover Euler’s function properties [S.6], zero-density results for automorphic $L$-functions [S.6], and the development of new semi-inner products in $p$-summable sequence spaces [S.7].

### Editorial Team
The volume was edited by three scholars specializing in algebraic and computational number theory [S.8]:

Diana Savin, an Associate Professor at Transilvania University of Brașov, focuses on ramification theory, associative algebra, and combinatorics [S.8].

Nicușor Minculete, also of Transilvania University of Brașov, specializes in mathematical inequalities, number theory, and geometry [S.8].

Vincenzo Acciaro, an Associate Professor at the University of Pescara, emphasizes local-global methods in number theory and associative algebra [S.8].

This document serves as an editorial for a special issue of *Mathematics* (2024), focusing on research in algebraic, analytic, and computational number theory.

### Core Disciplines
*   **Analytic Number Theory:** Employs mathematical analysis to study integers. Key areas include multiplicative and additive number theory. Bernhard Riemann’s work on the Riemann zeta function is foundational for understanding prime distribution. Typical tasks include the enumeration of primes and solutions to Diophantine equations.
*   **Algebraic Number Theory:** Investigates the arithmetic of algebraic number fields, specifically the ring of integers. It explores ideals, units, and the failure of unique factorization, which is quantified by the class number (the size of the ideal class group).

### Research Scope
The issue collects interdisciplinary research from international contributors. Key topics addressed include:
*   **Theoretical Foundations:** Ramification theory, class field theory, $L$-functions, modular forms, and elliptic curves.
*   **Applied Domains:** Associative and logical algebras, combinatorics, difference equations, and algebraic hyper-structures.

### Highlighted Contributions
The editorial details 17 specific research papers, including:
*   **Pseudoprimes:** Investigation of Fibonacci and Lucas pseudoprimes of level $k$ ([S.13]).
*   **Fibonacci sequences:** Analysis of generalized Fibonacci numbers, including their expression as sums of factorials and properties of $k$-Fibonacci quaternions ([S.13, S.14]).
*   **Difference Equations:** Global and local analysis of piecewise linear systems ([S.13]).
*   **Number Theoretic Functions:** Inequalities involving Euler’s function and the $\tau$ function within algebraic number fields ([S.13]).
*   **Zero-Density Estimates:** Development of new results for automorphic $L$-functions of $GL(n)$ ([S.13]).
*   **Algebraic Structures:** Characterization of residuated lattices with Noetherian spectra and explorations of ordered $n$-ary semihypergroups ([S.13, S.14]).
*   **Cryptography:** Development of authentication protocols utilizing Diophantine equations ([S.14]).
*   **Density and Summations:** Studies on the density of fractional parts of sequences and character sum bounds for Dirichlet characters ([S.15]).

The provided text, spanning pages [S.20] through [S.24], focuses on the mathematical investigation of generalized Lucas and Fibonacci pseudoprimes.

**Key Definitions and Background**
The authors define the generalized Lucas sequence $U_n(a, b)$ and the generalized Pell-Lucas sequence $V_n(a, b)$ via recursion formulas. Specific conditions for these sequences, such as $b = \pm 1$, are central to their analysis. The text references Legendre and Jacobi symbols to define pseudoprimality for composite numbers that behave similarly to primes under these sequences.

**Pseudoprimality Concepts**
The paper establishes several criteria for pseudoprimes:
* Generalized Lucas pseudoprime: An odd composite $n$ where $\gcd(n, b) = 1$ and $n$ divides $U_{n - (\frac{D}{n})}$.
* Weak generalized Lucas pseudoprime: A composite $n$ such that $n$ divides $U_{2n} - 1$.
* Generalized Bruckman-Lucas pseudoprime: A composite $n$ such that $n$ divides $V_n - a$.

**Fibonacci and Lucas Pseudoprimes of Level k**
A significant portion of the work is dedicated to Fibonacci pseudoprimes of level $k$, defined as composite numbers $n$ that satisfy $n | F_{kn - (\frac{n}{5})} - F_{k-1}$. The authors:
1. Provide a corrected version of a statement from previous literature (Proposition 2) regarding the relationship between pseudoprimes of different levels.
2. Formally disprove the claim that if $n$ is a Fibonacci pseudoprime of level 1 ($F_1$) and satisfies $n | F_{2n} - 1$, then $n$ must necessarily be a pseudoprime of all levels $k \geq 1$.
3. Provide counterexamples (such as in Example 1) to show that the relationships between these sets are more complex and not universally applicable.

**Methodology**
The authors utilize Binet-type formulas, bivariate cyclotomic polynomials, and congruence relations (notably involving the Legendre symbol) to explore these sequences. They note the contribution of new integer sequences to the Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (OEIS) and describe the use of computational tools like Matlab and Wolfram Alpha for these proofs and simulations.

The section spanning pages 27 to 36 (corresponding to the work by Eva Trojovská and Pavel Trojovský) focuses on the representation of Fibonacci numbers of order $r$ as the sum of consecutive factorial numbers. 

Key points from this research include:

The authors investigate the mathematical conditions under which a Fibonacci number of order $r$ (denoted as $F_n^{(r)}$) can be written as a sum of the form $\sum_{i=k}^{m} i!$.

The study provides an analytical approach to determine the existence and constraints of such representations. 

The paper connects classical sequence theory (Fibonacci numbers of order $r$) with factorial series, contributing to the broader field of computational and algebraic number theory as presented in the special issue. 

This work highlights the researchers' efforts to solve diophantine-type problems by bridging different areas of discrete mathematics and number theory.

The section spanning pages 37 to 64 corresponds to the article titled "Global and Local Behavior of the System of Piecewise Linear Difference Equations $x_{n+1} = |x_n| - y_n - b$ and $y_{n+1} = x_n - |y_n| + 1$ Where $b \geq 4$," authored by Busakorn Aiewcharoen, Ratinan Boonklurb, and Nanthiya Konglawan.

Key points of this research include:

The authors investigate the behavior of a specific system of two-dimensional piecewise linear difference equations defined by the relations:
$x_{n+1} = |x_n| - y_n - b$
$y_{n+1} = x_n - |y_n| + 1$
where $b \geq 4$ is a constant parameter.

The study provides a comprehensive analysis of both the global and local dynamics of this system. It examines the long-term behavior of the sequences $\{x_n\}$ and $\{y_n\}$ generated by these equations for various initial conditions and values of $b$.

The findings contribute to the broader understanding of piecewise linear systems, which are significant in various applications of number theory and computational mathematics. The analysis focuses on identifying fixed points, potential periodicity, and the overall stability characteristics of the system under the specified constraint for the constant $b$.

The section covering pages 65–74 corresponds to the article "Some Properties of Euler’s Function and of the Function $\tau$ and Their Generalizations in Algebraic Number Fields" by Nicușor Minculete and Diana Savin. 

Key points from this research include:

1. Theoretical Scope: The authors investigate generalizations of classical number-theoretic functions—specifically Euler’s totient function and the divisor function $\tau$—within the context of algebraic number fields.

2. Functional Properties: The study explores how these functions behave when extended from the set of natural numbers to the ring of integers of an algebraic number field. This involves analyzing the arithmetic structure of ideals and their norms.

3. Generalizations: The authors focus on extending established identities and properties of $\phi(n)$ and $\tau(n)$ to ensure they remain consistent or yield new insights when applied to generalized settings, such as evaluating these functions based on the prime ideal factorization in a given field.

4. Objective: The primary goal is to provide a comprehensive view of how these fundamental arithmetic functions adapt to higher-level algebraic structures, contributing to the mathematical understanding of prime distribution and divisor counting in non-classical domains.

The section spanning pages 75–96 contains the article titled "Regularities in Ordered $n$-Ary Semihypergroups" by Jukkrit Daengsaen and Sorasak Leeratanavalee.

The key points of this research are as follows:

The authors explore the algebraic structure of $n$-ary semihypergroups. In this study, an ordered $n$-ary semihypergroup is defined as a structure where the underlying set is equipped with an $n$-ary hyperoperation and a partial order relation that is compatible with this hyperoperation.

The research focuses on identifying regularities within these structures. The authors establish characterization theorems for ordered $n$-ary semihypergroups, specifically focusing on the notions of regular and intra-regular elements.

The study provides an analysis of how these elements behave under the given order relation. By examining the properties of $n$-ary hyperideals, the authors derive conditions under which specific subsets of the semihypergroup satisfy the criteria for regularity.

The findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the hierarchical classification of $n$-ary algebraic systems, providing tools to analyze structural regularities that extend the traditional theory of semihypergroups to $n$-ary and ordered contexts.

The section covering pages 97 to 110 contains the article "New Zero-Density Results for Automorphic L-Functions of $$GL(n)$$" by Wenjing Ding, Huafeng Liu, and Deyu Zhang (p. 97).

The key points of this research are:

The study focuses on the distribution of zeros for automorphic L-functions associated with the general linear group $$GL(n)$$.

The authors establish new zero-density estimates, which provide bounds on the number of zeros of these functions within specific regions of the critical strip.

These results contribute to the broader goals of analytic number theory, specifically in understanding the properties of L-functions and their implications for the distribution of prime numbers or similar arithmetic objects.

The work utilizes advanced analytic techniques to refine existing bounds for high-rank groups, extending the mathematical understanding of the zero-free regions and density theorems in the context of automorphic forms.

This section of the special issue comprises a collection of research articles covering various advanced topics in number theory and related mathematical fields. The key areas of focus include:

**Number Theory and Special Sequences**
Several articles investigate properties of sequences, including Fibonacci and Lucas-related structures. Highlights include:
* Studies on Fibonacci finite operator quaternions, Pauli Gaussian Fibonacci/Lucas quaternions, and LeonardoHybrid numbers (p. 111, 181, 285).
* Research on $k$-Fibonacci numbers and $k$-generalized Fibonacci sequences (p. 191).
* Examinations of generalized Lucas pseudoprimality and the classification of telescopic numerical semigroups (p. 9, 171).

**Algebraic and Analytic Number Theory**
This section explores deep structural properties of algebraic systems: 
* Divisibility of class numbers in imaginary quadratic fields (p. 151).
* The indices of septic number fields defined by trinomials $x^{7} + ax + b$ (p. 255).
* A generalized Bohr–Jessen type theorem concerning the Epstein zeta-function (p. 139).
* Investigation into the coefficients of inverse cyclotomic polynomials and the density of specific sequences modulo 1 (p. 207, 239).

**Computational and Applied Mathematics**
The contributors address practical applications and computational properties of algebraic structures:
* The development of authentication protocols utilizing quadratic Diophantine equations (p. 161).
* Applications of generalized Fibonacci sequences to elliptic curve cryptography (p. 267).
* Theoretical developments in residuated lattices, $n$-ary semihypergroups, and the construction of new semi-inner products in $p$-summable sequence spaces (p. 75, 125, 225).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the primary role of the 'class number' in the study of algebraic number fields?
options: It calculates the exact number of prime factors in a ring of integers.
It provides a measure of how much a ring of integers deviates from being a unique factorization domain.
It determines the total number of units present in a given algebraic number field.
It acts as a classification index for the ramification of ideals within a field.
questions.explanation: The text defines the class number as the order of the ideal class group of a ring of integers, which measures how far the ring of integers is from being a unique factorization domain.

questions.question: According to the editorial, what is a fundamental characteristic that distinguishes analytic number theory from algebraic number theory?
options: Analytic number theory is not concerned with prime numbers, while algebraic number theory is.
Analytic number theory relies on methods from mathematical analysis, whereas algebraic number theory studies the arithmetic of algebraic number fields.
Algebraic number theory is only applicable to quadratic fields, while analytic methods apply to all fields.
There is no functional difference between the two; they are different names for the same field.
questions.explanation: The text notes that analytic number theory inherits methods from mathematical analysis to solve integer problems, whereas algebraic number theory focuses on the arithmetic of algebraic number fields and their rings of integers.

questions.question: Based on the summary of the work by Aiewcharoen et al., what happens to the system of equations x_{n+1} = |x_n| - y_n - b and y_{n+1} = x_n - |y_n| + 1 when b increases to 6 or greater?
options: The solutions always converge to an equilibrium point.
The system becomes chaotic and never settles.
The solution converges to a periodic solution of prime period 5.
The system enters a state of logarithmic divergence.
questions.explanation: The editorial states that for the system of difference equations, if b >= 6, the solution becomes a periodic solution of prime period 5.

questions.question: Why is the study of the structure of the group of units in a ring of integers significant for arithmetic in a number field?
options: Units represent the primes of the field.
Factorization of algebraic integers is defined up to multiplicative units, necessitating an understanding of this structure.
Units are required to compute the derivative of the Riemann zeta function.
Units determine the class number of the field, which is otherwise incalculable.
questions.explanation: The editorial explains that because factorization is defined 'up to multiplicative units', understanding the group of units is essential to fully understand the arithmetic of the number field.

questions.question: What common approach is mentioned in the text for defining factorization in a number field?
options: Factorization is defined directly on the field elements without needing a subring.
Factorization is defined by examining the limit of the Epstein zeta-function.
Factorization is only possible by resurrecting the ring of integers within the number field.
Factorization is achieved strictly through the use of Pauli Gaussian quaternions.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'Factorization in a field makes sense only with respect to a subring, and so, we must resurrect the ring of integers in a number field in order to define it.'

cards.front: What are the two major areas of analytic number theory?
cards.back: Multiplicative number theory and additive number theory.

cards.front: What is the primary contribution of Bernhard Riemann to analytic number theory?
cards.back: He investigated the Riemann zeta function and established its importance for understanding the distribution of prime numbers.

cards.front: What does the 'class number' of a number field measure?
cards.back: It measures how far the ring of integers of a number field is from being a unique factorization domain (UFD).

cards.front: What are the two standard approaches to studying algebraic number theory?
cards.back: One approach uses ideals, and the other uses valuations.

cards.front: What is the definition of the class number of a number field?
cards.back: It is defined as the order of the ideal class group of its ring of integers.

cards.front: What is represented by the generalized Fibonacci number of order r, denoted t(r)n?
cards.back: It is a sequence defined by the recurrence t(r)n = t(r)n-1 + ... + t(r)n-r for n ≥ r, with initial values t(r)0 = 0 and t(r)i = 1 for 1 ≤ i ≤ r.

cards.front: What types of problems are typically addressed in analytic number theory?
cards.back: The enumeration of number-theoretic objects such as primes, solutions of Diophantine equations, and the distribution of integers.

cards.front: What is the global behavior of the system xn+1 = |xn| - yn - b and yn+1 = xn - |yn| + 1 when b ≥ 6?
cards.back: The solution becomes a periodic solution of prime period 5.

cards.front: Why is it necessary to understand the structure of the group of units in a ring of integers?
cards.back: Because any factorization of an algebraic integer is defined up to multiplicative units; understanding these is essential for understanding the field's arithmetic.

cards.front: What research area studies the arithmetic of algebraic number fields, including rings of integers and ideal factorization?

slides.title: Number Theory and Applications
bullets: Explores algebraic, analytic, and computational methods.
Covers research on arithmetic structures and number sequences.
slides.notes: This lecture introduces the multidisciplinary landscape of modern number theory as presented in the Special Issue.

slides.title: Foundations: Analytic and Algebraic
bullets: Analytic approach: Using analysis to study integer distribution.
Algebraic approach: Examining rings, ideals, and class numbers.
Riemann zeta function aids in prime number theory.
Class numbers reveal departures from unique factorization.
slides.notes: We focus on how analysis and algebra provide distinct, complementary lenses for examining number-theoretic problems.

slides.title: Generalized Lucas Pseudoprimality
bullets: Sequences defined by recurrence relations like U(n) and V(n).
Binet-type formulas link sequences to quadratic roots.
Used to define and study novel pseudoprime classes.
Legendre symbols help establish arithmetic congruences (p. 21).
slides.notes: These sequences extend classical concepts like Fibonacci numbers to broader algebraic categories and primality testing.
